task.title: Network Connectivity Test
task.description: Execute ping to default gateway to verify basic connectivity and latency.

solution.approach: Determine the default gateway IP address using system routing information, then assess whether a ping test is feasible and what metrics (latency, packet loss) are relevant for connectivity verification.
solution.algorithm: Use `ip route` or `route -n` to extract the default gateway, validate its presence, and confirm it's reachable via local network interface.

solution.approach: Design a simple ping command that sends a small number of packets (e.g., 4) to the default gateway with appropriate options to measure round-trip time and detect packet loss without flooding the network.
solution.algorithm: Execute: `ping -c 4 <gateway_ip>` where `<gateway_ip>` is obtained from step 1. This ensures controlled traffic and provides latency statistics.

solution.approach: Run the ping command as designed in step 2 and capture its output directly to analyze connectivity status and latency values.
solution.algorithm: Invoke `run_shell_command` with the constructed ping command string; do not redirect output to a file but parse stdout inline if needed.

solution.approach: Interpret the ping results: check for successful replies (no 'Destination Host Unreachable' or 'Request timed out'), compute average/min/max RTT from summary lines, and confirm less than 100% packet loss.
solution.algorithm: Parse the final statistics line of the ping output (e.g., 'rtt min/avg/max/mdev') and verify that avg latency is within acceptable bounds (< 500ms) and packet loss is low (< 10%).

solution.approach: Determine the default gateway IP address using system routing information, then assess whether a ping test is feasible and what metrics (latency, packet loss) are relevant for connectivity verification.
solution.algorithm: Use `ip route` or `route -n` to extract the default gateway, validate its presence, and confirm it's reachable via local network interface.

solution.approach: Design a simple ping command that sends a small number of packets (e.g., 4) to the default gateway with appropriate options to measure round-trip time and detect packet loss without flooding the network.
solution.algorithm: Execute: `ping -c 4 <gateway_ip>` where `<gateway_ip>` is obtained from step 1. This ensures controlled traffic and provides latency statistics.

task.title: External Reachability Check
task.description: Perform curl request to a public endpoint (e.g., http://example.com) to validate internet access.

solution.approach: Analyze the task requirements to determine if a simple HTTP GET request using curl is sufficient to validate external reachability. The goal is to confirm that the system has outbound internet access by attempting to connect to a reliable public endpoint such as http://example.com.
solution.algorithm: Use curl with appropriate flags to send a HEAD or GET request and capture response headers without downloading full content. Set a short timeout to avoid hanging on unresponsive endpoints.
solution.complexity: O(1) - Constant time operation involving a single network I/O call.

solution.approach: Design a robust curl command that checks connectivity while minimizing side effects. Use --head to fetch only headers, --max-time 5 to limit execution time, --silent to suppress progress output, and --fail to ensure non-2xx responses are treated as errors. This avoids unnecessary data transfer and provides clear success/failure indication.
solution.algorithm: Command: curl --head --silent --fail --max-time 5 http://example.com

solution.approach: Execute the designed curl command via run_shell_command. Capture stdout and stderr directly into variables for later validation. Do not redirect output to files; instead, use command substitution or direct assignment to preserve structured handling.
solution.algorithm: run_shell_command(curl --head --silent --fail --max-time 5 http://example.com)

solution.approach: Validate the outcome of the curl request by checking the exit code. A zero exit code indicates successful HTTP response (typically 2xx), confirming external reachability. Non-zero indicates failure due to network issues, DNS resolution failure, or server error. Log result accordingly.
solution.algorithm: if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then echo 'Success: External endpoint reachable'; else echo 'Failure: No internet access or endpoint unreachable'; fi

solution.approach: Analyze the task requirements to determine if a simple HTTP GET request using curl is sufficient to validate external reachability. The goal is to confirm that the system has outbound internet access by attempting to connect to a reliable public endpoint such as http://example.com.
solution.algorithm: Use curl with appropriate flags to send a HEAD or GET request and capture response headers without downloading full content. Set a short timeout to avoid hanging on unresponsive endpoints.
solution.complexity: O(1) - Constant time operation involving a single network I/O call.

task.title: DNS Resolution Validation
task.description: Use curl with DNS lookup to confirm name resolution is functioning correctly.

solution.approach: Analyze the task requirements to understand that DNS resolution validation involves testing whether a domain name can be resolved to an IP address using curl with DNS lookup capabilities. The goal is to confirm that name resolution is functioning correctly in the current environment.
solution.algorithm: Identify target domains for testing (e.g., google.com, example.com), determine appropriate curl flags for DNS resolution (--resolve or --connect-to), and plan how to interpret successful vs failed resolution.

solution.approach: Design a curl command that performs DNS resolution without actually making an HTTP request, using the --resolve flag to specify a custom IP for a domain, combined with -w '%{time_namelookup}' to measure and display the name resolution time and success status.
solution.algorithm: Use curl with --resolve to force DNS resolution of a test domain, include verbose output control via -sS to show errors but suppress normal progress, and format output to clearly indicate resolution outcome.

solution.approach: Execute curl with DNS resolution flags against a reliable public domain (google.com) to validate that name-to-IP mapping works correctly. Use relative path context and avoid file redirection by capturing stdout directly.
solution.algorithm: Run: curl -sS --resolve google.com:443:172.217.16.142 https://google.com -w '%{time_namelookup}' --max-time 10. Capture and return the full output including timing information.

solution.approach: Validate that the curl command executed successfully and returned a non-zero exit code indicating successful DNS resolution (since we're not completing the full request due to --max-time). Check that the %{time_namelookup} field shows a numeric value indicating successful resolution.
solution.algorithm: Parse the curl output to verify it contains a valid time value (not '0' or empty), and confirm curl exited with code 0 despite early termination due to max-time. Return success if both conditions met.

solution.approach: Analyze the task requirements to understand that DNS resolution validation involves testing whether a domain name can be resolved to an IP address using curl with DNS lookup capabilities. The goal is to confirm that name resolution is functioning correctly in the current environment.
solution.algorithm: Identify target domains for testing (e.g., google.com, example.com), determine appropriate curl flags for DNS resolution (--resolve or --connect-to), and plan how to interpret successful vs failed resolution.

task.description: Use curl with DNS lookup to confirm name resolution is functioning correctly.

artifacts.code: curl --resolve google.com:80:142.250.191.78 -I http://google.com/ && echo 'DNS resolution test completed'
